metadata.summary: Structured PM tool migration covering pre-migration audit, field mapping, ETL execution, and post-cutover validation with three-run strategy and legacy ID preservation.

content.title: Cross-Tool Migration Basics
content.summary: A structured approach to migrating project management data between tools, covering pre-migration audit, field mapping, ETL execution, and post-cutover validation. The critical rule: run the migration three times — dev test, full dry-run with rollback, real cutover — and preserve source IDs in a legacy_id custom field on the target so every external link and commit message that hardcoded PROJ-1234 still resolves.

why.p: Tool migrations routinely underestimate hidden costs: field semantics rarely map 1:1, attachment size limits silently drop files mid-migration, and comment threads lose ordering when user IDs don't resolve in the target. Migration cost (data, automations, integrations, training, parallel-run period) is frequently 30-50% of year-1 TCO — quantifying this before committing is the methodology's primary value.

Field semantics rarely map 1:1; "Priority: High" in Jira ≠ "Priority: High" in Linear if scales differ. Attachment size limits and rate limits silently drop files mid-migration. Comment threads lose ordering or @mentions when user IDs don't resolve in the target. Custom Jira plugins (Tempo, Structure, ScriptRunner) have no target equivalent — features get lost. "Reporter" / "Created date" fields are usually system-managed in the target → audit trail gap. ID changes (PROJ-1234 → ABC-5) break every external link, commit message, and bookmark.

list.item: Switching PM tools (Jira to Linear, Trello to ClickUp, Asana to Notion).
Consolidating two or more tools after acquisition or department merge.
Outgrowing a starter tool (Trello/Notion) onto an enterprise tool (Jira/Azure DevOps).
Compliance push forces migration to a tool with stronger security posture.
Current vendor's pricing model becomes hostile (per-seat hike, mandatory tier upgrade).
One person and fewer than 100 issues — re-create them by hand in an afternoon.
Vendor offers a guided managed migration that already handles data ETL — use it; this brief adds overhead.
Tool change is driven by hype rather than measurable pain — wait three months and re-evaluate.
Active product launch or freeze period — defer until after.
Source data is too dirty to be worth migrating; archive and start fresh.

section.name: pre-migration-audit
section.p: Complete a pre-migration audit before any data movement to uncover hidden costs and blocking issues.

rule.statement: Complete a pre-migration audit before any data movement: count total issues, attachments (by byte), custom fields, automation rules, and integrations; classify each integration as critical or non-critical.
rule.rationale: Skipping the audit produces mid-migration surprises — attachment size limits exceed target storage, custom fields have no target equivalent, critical integrations break with no rollback plan.

example.title: Pre-migration audit checklist
example.code: audit_checklist: data_inventory: total_projects: count total_issues: count total_attachments_gb: size custom_fields: list automations: count integrations: list usage_patterns: active_users: count key_workflows: list power_users: list pain_points: list dependencies: ci_cd: [tool, criticality] slack: [channels, automations] documentation_links: [wiki, confluence] compliance: audit_requirements: yes_or_no data_retention: policy access_controls: requirements

section.name: field-mapping-rules
section.p: Field mapping is the core of any migration strategy. Each source field must have an explicit decision — map, transform, or document as "won't migrate".

rule.statement: Every source field must be explicitly mapped to a target field OR explicitly marked as "won't migrate" with a documented reason; unmapped fields that silently drop data create post-cutover blame cycles.
rule.rationale: Documenting every "won't migrate" decision pre-empts post-cutover disputes about missing data. Silence is not agreement.

rule.statement: Preserve source IDs in a "legacy_id" custom field on the target tool; external systems that hardcoded source issue IDs (commit messages, bookmarks, documentation links) still resolve.
rule.rationale: ID changes (PROJ-1234 → ABC-5) break every external link simultaneously. The legacy_id field is the only safe bridge.

example.title: Field mapping with won't-migrate documentation
example.code: | Source (Jira) | Target (Linear) | Transformation | Won't Migrate | |------------------|------------------|-----------------------|---------------| | Summary | Title | Direct copy | No | | Description | Description | Markdown convert | No | | Issue Type | Label | Bug→bug, Story→feat | No | | Status | State | See status mapping | No | | Priority | Priority | See priority mapping | No | | Sprint | Cycle | Match by date range | No | | Epic Link | Project | Create Linear projects| No | | Reporter | — | System-managed | Yes — log | | Custom Plugin X | — | No equivalent | Yes — archive |

section.name: status-and-priority-mapping
section.p: Status and priority scales vary between tools and require careful explicit mapping to prevent silent data corruption.

rule.statement: Map status fields explicitly with a lookup table showing source state, target state, and any behavioral differences; "Resolved" in Jira may mean approved-not-yet-deployed while the target tool's "Done" means shipped.
rule.rationale: Status semantics rarely map 1:1. Reviewing each mapping for behavioral differences prevents silent reinterpretation of work state.

example.title: Status and priority mapping tables
example.code:
status_mapping: "Open": "Backlog" "To Do": "Todo" "In Progress": "In Progress" "In Review": "In Review" "Done": "Done" "Won't Do": "Canceled"
priority_mapping: "Highest": "Urgent" "High": "High" "Medium": "Medium" "Low": "Low" "Lowest": "No Priority"
example.note: Status semantics rarely map 1:1. Review each mapping for behavioral differences: "Resolved" in Jira means approved-not-yet-deployed; check whether the target tool has an equivalent state or if items should map to "Done".

section.name: three-run-migration-strategy
section.p: Run every migration three times to catch different classes of bug at each level: logic errors, scale issues, and live-load issues.

rule.statement: Run the migration three times: dev-environment test, full dry-run with rollback on a clone of production, real cutover; each run catches a different class of bug.
rule.rationale: Dev test catches logic errors. Full dry-run catches rate limits, size limits, and data-edge cases that only appear at scale. Real cutover catches timing and concurrency issues under live load.

list.item: Run 1 (dev test): validate field mappings, auth, and basic ETL flow on a small data sample.
Run 2 (dry-run): run against a full production data clone; validate counts, comments, and attachments; confirm rollback path.
Run 3 (real cutover): freeze source, execute migration, validate three numbers, declare success or rollback.

section.name: freeze-period
section.p: Freezing the source tool during cutover prevents reconciliation complexity from issues created mid-migration.

rule.statement: Freeze the source tool 4 hours before cutover; route new tickets created during the window to a known "intake" queue and re-key them by hand after cutover.
rule.rationale: Issues created during migration create reconciliation complexity. A short freeze with a manual intake queue is simpler and less risky than attempting live-sync.

section.name: post-cutover-validation
section.p: Validate migration success at three independent levels: aggregate counts, sample inspection, and integration testing.

rule.statement: Validate three numbers before declaring migration successful: total issue count, total comment count, and total attachment volume by byte.
rule.rationale: Three independent counts at different granularities catch distinct failure modes: issues lost, comments orphaned, and attachments silently dropped at size limits.

rule.statement: Manually sample-validate 50 random issues by hand, comparing source to target field-by-field; automated count validation does not catch field-content corruption.
rule.rationale: Status mapping bugs, Markdown-to-HTML conversion failures, and @mention resolution failures appear in field content, not in counts.

example.title: Post-migration validation with issue sampling
example.note: After migrating 12,000 Jira issues to Linear: count matched (12,000 = 12,000); comment count matched (47,831 = 47,831); attachment bytes matched (8.4 GB = 8.4 GB). Manual sample of 50 issues revealed 3 with status mapping errors (Resolved → Done instead of In Review). Fix applied; re-run of status correction script; re-validate.

section.name: parallel-run-and-rollback
section.p: Plan for continued access to the source tool and a reversible rollback path during the transition.

rule.statement: Keep the source tool read-only for at least 30 days after cutover; users will need to look up historical context for months after the switch.
rule.rationale: Post-cutover lookup requests are predictable and frequent. Read-only access at no cost is safer than full decommission and cheaper than rebuilding access when needed.

antipattern.description: Decommissioning the source tool immediately after cutover success.
antipattern.reason: "Inevitably someone needs to look up what was decided in PROJ-456" — this is guaranteed to happen within days. Premature decommission forces re-provisioning under pressure.

section.name: agentic-workflows
section.p: When using agents or subagents to assist migrations, follow these patterns and watch for these failure modes.

rule.statement: Human-in-the-loop checkpoints are mandatory at: mapping approval, cutover go/no-go decision, and rollback decisions.
rule.rationale: Each is an irreversible or high-impact decision. Agent errors at these gates compound into expensive remediation.

example.title: Field-mapper subagent prompt pattern
example.code: You are field-mapper. Inputs: source_schema.json, target_schema.json, org_policy.md. Output a Markdown table with columns: source_field, target_field, transformation, ambiguity_flag. Where mapping is lossy or ambiguous, set ambiguity_flag=YES and propose two alternatives. Do not invent target fields not in source_schema.json.

list.item: migration-auditor (sonnet) — runs pre-migration audit, returns YAML with counts, custom fields, integrations.
field-mapper (sonnet) — proposes source-to-target field mappings and lookup tables; flags ambiguities for human decision.
dry-run-extractor (haiku) — calls source API read-only, validates auth, counts records, exports to JSONL.
mapping-validator (haiku) — checks mapping coverage: 100% of source fields either mapped or explicitly dropped.

antipattern.description: Agents running migration without a resumable checkpoint file per issue ID.
antipattern.reason: Rate limits and network failures crash mid-batch migrations. Without a checkpoint file (last successfully migrated ID), the entire run must restart from the beginning.

antipattern.description: Agents migrating @mentions without user ID resolution.
antipattern.reason: Source and target user IDs differ. Unresolved @mentions silently become plain text or broken links. Resolve user ID mapping in advance; log and drop unresolvable mentions.

antipattern.description: Accepting an LLM-estimated migration cost without a 2x multiplier.
antipattern.reason: LLM migration estimates are systematically low; they omit training, parallel-run period, and integration rebuild cost. Multiply by 2x minimum and add a parallel-run period.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Reference tooling and services available for migration execution.

list.item: jira-cli (ankitpokhrel) — Jira export, JQL, batch mutations. https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli
Linear CLI / GraphQL — Linear ingestion via official GraphQL API. https://developers.linear.app
gh CLI — GitHub Projects v2 (GraphQL) for source or target. https://cli.github.com
notion-cli (community) — Notion exports/imports for migration drafts. https://github.com/jakeswenson/notion
csvkit — Slice/dice large issue CSV exports during mapping. pip install csvkit
jq — Transform JSONL exports between API shapes. https://stedolan.github.io/jq/
dataset (Python) — Quick SQLite staging of intermediate rows. pip install dataset
Atlassian JCMA (SaaS, managed) — Use for Jira Server→Cloud only; no agent hooks.
Linear Importer (SaaS) — Decent Jira/Asana coverage; no API to drive it.
Plandek / Unito (SaaS sync) — Two-way sync during parallel-run phase, not full migration. REST API available.
Project Migrator (Solidify, SaaS) — Azure DevOps ↔ Jira, supports CMMI; non-trivial cost.
Exalate (SaaS) — Sync rules between Jira/Zendesk/ServiceNow during cutover. Groovy scripts.
Backbone (community, OSS) — Open-source Jira→GitHub Issues; hackable. Node.
ClickUp Importer (SaaS) — Zapier or built-in only.
Run the migration three times: dev test, full dry-run with rollback, real cutover. Each catches a different class of bug.
Freeze the source 4 hours before cutover; new tickets created during the window go to a known "intake" queue and get re-keyed by hand.
Preserve source IDs in a "legacy_id" custom field on the target — every external system that hardcoded PROJ-123 still resolves.
Keep the source read-only for 30+ days. Inevitably someone needs to look up "what was decided in PROJ-456".
Validate three numbers before declaring success: total issues, total comments, total attachments by byte count.
Document every "won't migrate" decision; it pre-empts the post-cutover blame meeting.
Agents underestimate rate limits and crash mid-batch — always implement exponential backoff and resumable state (issue ID checkpoint file).
LLMs invent custom-field names confidently; constrain field-mapping output to the actual target schema, fail fast if hallucinated.
Comment migration breaks @mentions because user IDs differ; map users in advance, drop unresolved mentions, log them.
A "successful" migration with wrong status mapping silently buries 30% of work in the wrong column. Always sample-validate 50 random items by hand.
Token cost scales with issue count × description size; export to JSONL and let agents read summaries, not raw bodies.

template.purpose: Source-to-target field mapping table with transformation rules and ambiguity flags.
Compare source vs target issue counts post-migration; exit 1 if drift exceeds 1%.
